tutorial.heroEyebrow: User manual for AI coding tools like Claude and Codex
tutorial.tryWorkflowAdd: Add this workflow to my workspace
tutorial.heroTitle: The shortest path to building workflows
tutorial.heroDesc: A guide to quickly learn the real workflow: writing instructions, assembling workflows, executing, and reviewing results in Watermelon.
tutorial.s1Intro: The basic flow of Watermelon is simple. First create instructions, place them in a workflow, then check execution results on the tasks page.
tutorial.s1AttachmentsDesc: Attach scripts, reference docs, and config files to workflow nodes. Agents automatically download needed attachments during execution.
tutorial.s1Order1: 1. Define work units in Instructions first.
tutorial.s1Order2: 2. Assemble the execution order in Workflows.
tutorial.s1Order4: 4. Check progress and results in Tasks after execution.
tutorial.s1WhenToUse: What tasks are good for Watermelon
tutorial.s1Use1: Tasks that require repeating the same review procedures
tutorial.s1Use2: Multi-step tasks needing human approval or input midway
tutorial.s1Use3: Tasks where execution process and results need to be tracked later
tutorial.s1FoldersDesc: Workflows and instructions are organized in folders. Clicking a folder in the sidebar also shows items from its subfolders.
tutorial.s1VisPersonal: Personal — Only you can see it.
tutorial.s1VisGroup: Group — Shared with your group members.
tutorial.s1VisInherit: Inherit — Follows the parent folder's visibility.
tutorial.s1VisTip: You can quickly check visibility by the icon next to each folder.
tutorial.s2Intro: Instructions are the smallest unit of a workflow. It's best to have a single purpose and a single expected result per instruction.
tutorial.s2GoodText: Review the credential card list, check for duplicate service names and empty descriptions, and organize findings in a table.
tutorial.s2BadText: Review the entire system and fix everything as needed.
tutorial.s2Tip1: Write what to produce before writing what to judge.
tutorial.s2Tip2: Use specific criteria instead of vague expressions. e.g., 'no duplicate service names' instead of 'make it look nice'.
tutorial.s2Tip3: Specify the output format. e.g., bullet list, table, summary paragraph, file path.
tutorial.s3Intro: A workflow is a blueprint that connects instructions in order. Rather than adding more nodes, deciding where to place approval and repetition points is more important.
tutorial.s3ActionDesc: A standard execution step that runs automatically.
tutorial.s3GateDesc: An approval step that requires user confirmation or response.
tutorial.s3LoopDesc: A step that repeats until a condition is met.
tutorial.s3Rec2: 2. Action: Organize issues and improvement points.
tutorial.s3Rec4: 4. Action: Produce deliverables in the confirmed direction.
tutorial.s4Intro: Clicking the Run button on the workflow detail opens a 2-step dialog. Enter a title and context, then select your AI tool (Claude Code, Codex CLI, etc.) to generate a ready-to-paste command. Paste that command into your agent to create and execute the task.
tutorial.s4Pre1: If a step requires credentials, verify they are connected first.
tutorial.s4Pre2: If there are loop steps, ensure the exit condition is clear.
tutorial.s4Pre3: If there are approval steps, decide who will respond.
tutorial.s4HitlDesc: When a Gate node has HITL (Human-in-the-Loop) enabled, the agent waits for your approval after completing its work.
tutorial.s4Hitl1: An 'Awaiting Approval' indicator appears in the task detail.
tutorial.s4Hitl2: Review the result and click the approve button to proceed.
tutorial.s4Hitl3: If the result is insufficient, use rewind to re-execute from a previous step.
tutorial.s4RunningDesc: The current step is in progress and new logs keep accumulating.
tutorial.s4WaitingDesc: Waiting for user response or external conditions.
tutorial.s4FailedDesc: Check the cause and re-execute or rewind if needed.
tutorial.s5Intro: On the tasks page, you switch between list and detail views. The list is for a quick overview; the detail is for deep-diving into a single execution.
tutorial.s5ListTitle: What to see in the list view
tutorial.s5List3: What result came from the most recent step
tutorial.s5DetailTitle: What to see in the detail view
tutorial.s5Detail1: Left timeline: which steps are completed
tutorial.s5Detail2: Right result panel: deliverables, structured output, comments
tutorial.s5Detail3: For loop steps: difference between previous and latest iterations
tutorial.s6Intro: For first-time users, following a real operational flow is much easier than abstract explanations. Below is the most realistic example of creating a UI review workflow.
tutorial.s6Title: Try it: Credential card review workflow
tutorial.s6Step1_1: Review the current credential card UI and list issues
tutorial.s6Step1_2: Organize issues by importance and suggest fix priorities
tutorial.s6Step1_3: Re-review after fixes and summarize remaining issues
tutorial.s6Step2_2: 2. Gate: User confirms what to fix first
tutorial.s6Step2_3: 3. Action: Organize fix proposals in the chosen direction
tutorial.s6Step2_4: 4. Loop: Re-review after fixes and decide whether to end
tutorial.s6Step3Title: Step 3. Write the task context like this
tutorial.s6Step3Desc: It's better to be specific in the task context.
tutorial.s6Step3Example: Review the credential page card UI. Focus on duplicate service name display, action menu placement, description text density, and edit entry method, then organize fix priorities.
tutorial.s6Step4Title: Step 4. What to check in tasks after execution
tutorial.s6Step4_1: 1. Quickly check which workflow is in which state in the list.
tutorial.s6Step4_2: 2. Read each step's result and comments in the detail view.
tutorial.s6Step4_3: 3. Respond immediately when a Gate step appears to continue the flow.
tutorial.s6Step4_4: 4. If unsatisfied with results, rewind and proceed again.
tutorial.s7Intro: Connecting the Watermelon MCP server to your AI tool automates workflow execution. The agent performs what a human would do in the web UI.
tutorial.s7Setup1: Download the source, build the CLI, and link the local watermelon command.
tutorial.s7Setup2: Copy the token from the invite link sent by the admin.
tutorial.s7Setup3: Complete invite-based setup with watermelon accept <token> -s <serverURL>.
tutorial.s7Setup4: If you already have an API key, run watermelon init -p <profile> -s <serverURL> -k <apiKey> to connect directly or refresh MCP settings.
tutorial.s7Setup5: Run watermelon status to verify the active profile and connection.
tutorial.s7ToolStartWorkflow: Start a new task from a workflow ID.
tutorial.s7ToolAdvance: Get the next step's instruction information.
tutorial.s7ToolExecuteStep: Submit the execution result for the current step.
tutorial.s7ToolRequestApproval: Request human approval at a HITL step.
tutorial.s7ToolCompleteTask: Mark the task as complete when all steps are done.
tutorial.s7ToolRewind: Rewind to a previous step to re-execute.
tutorial.s7ToolHeartbeat: Report progress in real-time during execution.
tutorial.s7ToolListAttachments: List files attached to a workflow node.
tutorial.s7ToolUploadAttachment: Upload a file to a workflow node.
tutorial.s7Flow4: 4. Repeat 2-3. At HITL steps, call request_approval and wait for human approval
tutorial.s7AutoDesc: With MCP, you can also auto-generate instructions and workflows, or design loops that analyze execution results and improve them incrementally.
tutorial.s7Auto2: Analyze task logs to find frequently stuck steps.
tutorial.s7Auto3: Create improved instruction versions and compare results.
tutorial.s8Tip1Desc: It's more stable to first succeed with a short workflow of 3-4 steps, then gradually add approval or loop steps.
tutorial.s8Tip2Desc: If instructions of the same type always use the same result format, it becomes much easier to read and compare task logs later.
tutorial.s8Tip3Desc: Unless user response is truly needed for decision-making, ending with an Action is better for execution speed and usability.
tutorial.s8Tip4Desc: The more specific the workflow title, instruction title, and task context, the easier it is to read context from the list and detail breadcrumbs.
tutorial.s8CtaDesc: First create work units in Instructions, then assemble the sequence in Workflows. Results can be checked immediately in Tasks.
tutorial.sVsIntro: Enable visual_selection on a Gate node and the agent renders an HTML selection UI directly. The user clicks a button to respond — no typing required, and the workflow continues instantly.
tutorial.sVsHow1: When the agent reaches a Gate step, it writes bk-* component HTML.
tutorial.sVsHow2: A popup opens automatically on the task detail page.
tutorial.sVsHow3: The user clicks their choice and confirms.
tutorial.sVsHow4: The selection is sent back to the agent and the next step begins.
tutorial.sVsMore1: bk-cards — image card selection (for choosing layouts or chart types)
tutorial.sVsMore4: bk-code-compare — compare two versions of code
tutorial.sVsMore5: bk-split, bk-pros-cons, bk-mockup, bk-timeline — comparison and review layouts
tutorial.sVsTip: During wm-design or wm-start execution, the agent auto-generates VS UIs at Gate nodes. Just set visual_selection: true on the workflow node — no extra configuration needed.
tutorial.sSkIntro: Beyond the web UI, Watermelon can be used directly from your AI coding tool via slash commands (/skill). Skills are the fastest and most natural path to running workflows.
tutorial.sSkBkStart: Select a workflow and create/run a task. Also handles resuming and closing interrupted tasks.
tutorial.sSkBkDesign: Input a natural-language goal, design the workflow structure, and register it on the server.
tutorial.sSkBkApprove: Approve a HITL-pending step or submit a gate response from the CLI.
tutorial.sSkBkImprove: Analyze an existing workflow, create an improved version, and run a comparison.
tutorial.sSkBkNext: Find the active task and resume execution from the current step.
tutorial.sSkBkReport: Send bug reports and improvement feedback to the developer.
tutorial.sSkBkInstruction: Create, edit, and delete instruction templates, and link credentials.
tutorial.sSkBkHelp: Display all available wm-* commands with descriptions.
tutorial.sSkSession1: When /wm-start runs, tasks inactive for over 2 hours are automatically set to timed_out.
tutorial.sSkSession2: If there are running or timed_out tasks, you can choose: continue, close and start fresh, or start new while keeping existing.
tutorial.sSkSession3: If multiple tasks exist, the most recent one is used as the reference for your choices.
tutorial.sSkInterrupt1: Typing 'stop', 'quit', or similar during execution triggers an interruption dialog.
tutorial.sSkInterrupt2: Pause — keep the task in running state to resume later.
tutorial.sSkInterrupt3: Close task — calls complete_task and records it as 'closed' in history.
tutorial.s4TimedOutDesc: Automatically set after 2 hours of inactivity. Resume or close the task with /wm-start.
tutorial.s4FeedbackDesc: Once all steps complete, the agent runs a quick feedback survey before marking the task done. A simple rating and comment helps improve the workflow over time.
tutorial.s5RewindDesc: Press the rewind button at the bottom-left of the task detail to cancel the current step and re-run from the previous one. Use it when you're not satisfied with a result.
tutorial.s5CommentDesc: You can leave a comment below any step result. Both agents and humans can write comments, making it useful for recording per-step feedback and context.
tutorial.s5TimedOutDesc: Running tasks inactive for 2+ hours are automatically set to timed_out. They appear with a yellow badge in the list and can be resumed or closed with /wm-start.
tutorial.s7ToolSetVisualHtml: Register the HTML UI to render at a VS Gate node.
tutorial.s7ToolGetWebResponse: Poll for the user's VS click result.
tutorial.s7ToolSaveFeedback: Save feedback to the server before completing the task.
tutorial.s7ToolSubmitReport: Submit a developer feedback report to the server.
tutorial.s7FeedbackDesc: When the last workflow step finishes, the agent runs save_feedback → complete_task in order. It never calls complete_task directly without collecting feedback first.
